title: Figma - Product-Led Welcome
why_special: Purple gradients and product-mirroring design. The email feels like opening the app for the first time. Clear getting-started CTAs without clutter. Sets the bar for SaaS welcome emails.
what_to_steal: Mirror your product's visual language in your welcome email. Make onboarding steps feel like using the product, not reading about it.

title: Claude - Award-Winning Welcome Email
why_special: Won the Welcome Email category at Really Good Email Awards 2025. Judges praised the branding and clear sense of purpose. Sophisticated AI brand translated into warm, approachable email.
what_to_steal: Lead with purpose, not features. Even technical products should feel human in the inbox.
conversion_data: RGE 2025 Award Winner - Welcome Email category

title: Superhuman - Plain-Text Onboarding Series
why_special: Deliberately uses plain text instead of HTML. One email per day teaching a single feature. Feels like getting a note from a friend, not a marketing machine. Better deliverability too.
what_to_steal: Sometimes the best design is no design. Plain text can outperform polished HTML when the goal is trust and personal connection.

title: MoMA - Gallery-Quality Welcome
why_special: Treats the email itself as a piece of art. Gallery-quality imagery, restrained typography, generous white space. Non-commercial approach that makes you feel like a patron, not a customer.
what_to_steal: Use white space with the confidence of an art gallery. Let imagery breathe. Restraint is a design choice.

subject_line: Smiles, welcome to the Lyka Pack!
title: Lyka - Pet Name Personalization Welcome
why_special: Uses the pet's name in the subject line, not the owner's. Community language ('the Pack') builds belonging from first touch. Warm illustrations and personality-driven copy.
what_to_steal: Personalize with unexpected data points. The pet's name is more emotional than the owner's name. Build community language early.

title: Headspace - Mindful Onboarding
why_special: Distinctive cartoon characters. No sharp edges anywhere in the design for a softer, friendlier feel. Every color and shape is intentional. The email IS the brand experience. Tailored onboarding paths for active vs inactive users.
what_to_steal: Your email design should feel like using your product. If your product is calm, your email should be calm. Match the emotional register.
conversion_data: Welcome emails generate 4x more opens, 5x more clicks vs regular campaigns; 50% average open rate

title: Hyggekrog - Small Brand, 90% Open Rate
why_special: A small personal brand achieving a 90% open rate and 32.94% click rate. Purple background with clean layout. Founder personally writes every email. Proof that authenticity beats production value.
what_to_steal: Founder-written emails outperform corporate ones. Be a person. Set expectations clearly. Include a discount to drive first purchase.

title: Who Gives A Crap - Values-Driven Welcome
why_special: Creative footer design with playful brand icons and a sincere land acknowledgement. Social links spaced for breathing room. The footer alone builds brand respect. Even the parts nobody reads are beautiful.
what_to_steal: Design every element, including the footer. Values-driven messaging can differentiate even in the most unglamorous categories.

subject_line: Upgrade to the new iPad Air. Now supercharged by M4.
title: Apple - iPad Air M4 Launch
why_special: Masterclass in letting the product speak for itself. Stunning photography on clean background. Every pixel serves the product. Massive white space. The dual CTA (buy/learn more) gives choice without clutter.
what_to_steal: If your product looks good, get out of its way. One hero image, minimal copy, maximum impact.

title: Linear - The Gold Standard Changelog
why_special: The benchmark for SaaS product updates. Bold, concise headlines. GIFs showing features in action. Benefit-driven language, not jargon. Weekly summaries that people actually want to read.
what_to_steal: Use GIFs to show features, not describe them. Write headlines that describe the benefit, not the feature. Make changelogs feel like content, not documentation.

title: Gamma - AI Feature Launch
why_special: The subject line does all the work: product name + 'launches today.' Period. Clean tech-forward design with product screenshots. Urgent without being desperate.
what_to_steal: Short, declarative subject lines. Period at the end adds weight. Let the product screenshot be the hero, not illustrations.

title: Duolingo - Gamified Product Update
why_special: Character-led illustrations with gamified flow structure mirroring the app's progression levels. Product updates feel like play, not announcements. The beloved mascot carries the entire message.
what_to_steal: If you have a mascot or character, use it everywhere. Make product updates feel like unlocking a new level, not reading a memo.

title: Fridja - Pre-Release That Sold 25% of Stock
why_special: High-quality product imagery with limited-time discount and bold CTA. Simple, no-nonsense design. Sold 25% of initial stock before the official release. Proof that email pre-launch works.
what_to_steal: Give your email list early access. Simple product shot + clear discount + single CTA. Don't overcomplicate launch emails.
conversion_data: 25% of initial stock sold before release day

title: Snif - Sold-Out as Social Proof
why_special: Flips the script: leads with 'SOLD OUT' as the entire design concept. Scarcity becomes the message, not the tactic. Pre-order CTA follows after urgency is established. No customer testimonial is more powerful than 'sold out.'
what_to_steal: If your product sold out, lead with that. Social proof through scarcity is more powerful than any review. Then offer pre-order.

title: Starbucks - Seasonal Menu Drop
why_special: Vibrant pastel spring palette that makes you want to visit a store immediately. Multiple drink showcases in a clean grid. Product photography styled to match seasonal themes. Joy in a 600px column.
what_to_steal: Match your color palette to the season. Use product photography as the primary design element. Grid layouts work for multi-product showcases.

title: Patagonia - Purpose-Driven Newsletter
why_special: Combines product promotion with outdoor storytelling seamlessly. Editorial-quality photography. The subject line sells the weather, not the product. Commerce and purpose in perfect balance.
what_to_steal: Sell the context, not the product. 'Running gear for winter's worst' is about the runner, not the jacket. Story-driven layouts blend commerce with purpose.

title: theSkimm - Voice as the Entire Product
why_special: 3.5M+ subscribers (more than NYT digital). Tone IS the brand: funny phrases, movie quotes, casual language to make heavy news digestible. Proves voice matters more than visual design.
what_to_steal: Your newsletter's voice is more important than its design. Be opinionated. Be funny. Talk like a person. Consistency of voice builds audiences, not consistency of templates.

title: ARMRA - Deep-Dive Educational Email
why_special: Reads like a white paper but stays accessible. Citations and functional information. Saturated visuals with complementary fonts. Treats subscribers as intelligent readers. Q1 2025 standout on RGE.
what_to_steal: Go deep. Subscribers can handle complexity if the writing is good. Include citations. Treat your audience like adults. Education builds trust that converts later.

title: Howler Brothers - Personality-Rich Newsletter
why_special: Subject line sets the Texas drawl tone immediately. Rugged outdoor photography with playful, voice-rich copy. Sounds like a person, not a corporation. The brand equivalent of your coolest friend recommending gear.
what_to_steal: Your subject line should sound like your brand talks. Regional voice and personality beats polished corporate copy every time.

title: Collaborative Fund - Textured Bold-Color Newsletter
why_special: Limited palette of red and yellow with crumpled paper textures. Bold color bursts with clean horizontal divides. The texture makes a 2D email feel visceral and three-dimensional.
what_to_steal: Add texture to your emails via high-quality graphics. A limited, bold color palette is more memorable than a safe one. Texture creates depth.

title: Absolut - Wall-to-Wall Electric Blue
why_special: Electric blue from top to bottom with big, punchy typography. Pure brand consistency. The email IS Absolut. No compromise on identity. Unmistakable at inbox scan speed.
what_to_steal: Own a color. Use it everywhere. Your email should be recognizable at a glance without reading a single word.

title: Tracksmith - CEO Letter as Newsletter
why_special: Reads like a personal letter from a fellow runner, not a brand. Narrative-driven, storytelling over sales. Builds deep emotional connection through authentic voice. The CEO as newsletter personality.
what_to_steal: Have a human write your newsletter, not a marketing team. Personal letters outperform designed templates for building loyalty.

title: The Cut - Illustrations Instead of Photography
why_special: Quirky custom illustrations instead of stock photography. Brain-friendly simplicity with personality. Proof that newsletters don't need photos to be visually engaging. Illustration as differentiation.
what_to_steal: Custom illustration can differentiate your newsletter from every other photo-heavy email. Less realistic, more memorable.

title: Tuft & Needle - 3-Part Objection Handler
why_special: Three emails, three objections. Email 1: acknowledges mattress shopping pain points. Email 2: transparent competitor comparison. Email 3: satisfaction guarantee. Each links to a dedicated objection-handling landing page.
what_to_steal: Treat cart abandonment as a conversation, not a reminder. Address specific objections in sequence. Create landing pages for each objection.

subject_line: The Airlift 7/8 Decadent Bodysuit in your cart sold out
title: Alo Yoga - Scarcity as Cart Recovery
why_special: Tells the customer their item SOLD OUT. Creates urgency through scarcity without discounting. Premium brand approach to cart recovery that doesn't cheapen the brand. FOMO without the desperation.
what_to_steal: Scarcity notifications outperform discount offers for premium brands. 'It's gone' is more motivating than '10% off.'

title: Liquor Loot - Witty Subject Line Recovery
why_special: Subject line is everything: 'Your cart is sobering up.' Perfect brand-voice alignment. Witty and unexpected in a category full of generic 'You forgot something!' emails.
what_to_steal: Your cart abandonment subject line should sound like your brand. Humor improves open rates when it's authentic to the brand voice.

title: Ugmonk - Owner Personal Outreach Recovery
why_special: Direct message from the owner/designer. Uses first name, asks for feedback, offers a reply option. Feels like a genuine personal email, not marketing automation. The anti-template.
what_to_steal: Send cart abandonment emails from a real person. Include a reply option. Ask why they didn't buy. The data is worth more than the recovery.

title: Dollar Shave Club - Bear Image Social Proof
why_special: Bear covering its eyes. Customer testimonials as social proof. Laid-back language. The unexpected imagery stops the scroll, the testimonials close the sale. Nobody expects a bear in a cart abandonment email.
what_to_steal: Use unexpected imagery to stop the scroll. Pair it with customer testimonials. Casual language outperforms corporate copy in cart recovery.

title: Allbirds - Casual Cart Recovery
why_special: 'Howdy' as a cart abandonment opener. Clean product display with sustainability messaging woven in. Non-pushy, friendly, on-brand. Cart recovery that builds brand love, not resentment.
what_to_steal: Open with personality, not urgency. Weave your brand values (sustainability, quality) into recovery emails naturally.

title: Stripe - The Gold Standard Receipt
why_special: Universally cited as the benchmark for transactional email. 472px fixed width, Helvetica, primary color #676BE5. Minimalist aesthetic with careful visual hierarchy. Reads perfectly on every device. The receipt every developer aspires to build.
what_to_steal: Narrow width (472px vs standard 600px) improves readability. One font family. One accent color. Strategic CTA buttons. Design with engineer-level precision.

title: Airbnb - Premium Booking Confirmation
why_special: Turns a transactional moment into excitement. Property photos, host details, check-in info all presented cleanly. Reduces friction by making everything accessible without opening the app. The confirmation email as travel anticipation builder.
what_to_steal: Make transactional emails exciting, not boring. Include all essential details but style them to build anticipation. Property photos turn a confirmation into a countdown.

title: Haoma - Tree-Planting Order Confirmation
why_special: Order confirmation that weaves brand messaging about tree-planting into transaction details. Recycling note reinforces sustainability. Every touchpoint, even transactional, tells the brand story.
what_to_steal: Your brand mission belongs in transactional emails too. The post-purchase moment is when customers are most receptive to your values.

title: Webflow - Verification as Onboarding
why_special: Account verification email that includes a video tutorial. Turns a mandatory transactional moment into an onboarding opportunity. Why waste a guaranteed-open email on just a verification link?
what_to_steal: Verification emails have the highest open rates of any email. Add value beyond the verification link. Video tutorials, quick starts, welcome content.

title: Feastables - Interactive Trivia Game Email
why_special: Interactive trivia built with Spellbound.io directly in the email. Different flow screens based on user clicks. Encouraged email re-opens (unheard of). Gamification that actually works in email.
what_to_steal: Interactive elements inside emails drive engagement far beyond static content. Spellbound.io and AMP for email make this possible. Gamification encourages re-opens.
conversion_data: Engagement from thousands of subscribers; encouraged email re-opens

title: True Botanicals - Liquid Gold Hero Banner
why_special: Hero banner designed to mimic the texture of skincare oils. Golden-hued liquid design visually reinforces nourishment and glow. A/B tested against a flat banner and won decisively.
what_to_steal: Make your hero banner feel like the product. Texture and sensory design outperform flat graphics. Always A/B test your hero.
conversion_data: 20% higher click rate vs flat rectangular banner

title: Nike - Deliberately Sparse Curiosity Email
why_special: Deliberately sparse layout piques curiosity for a site launch. Minimal information builds intrigue. What you leave OUT of an email can be more powerful than what you put in. The negative space IS the design.
what_to_steal: Restraint is power. One image, minimal text, maximum curiosity. Not every email needs to say everything.

title: Brooklinen - Mystery Sale Unwrapping GIF
why_special: Mystery unwrapping GIF animation teases the discount without revealing it. Calendar app integration for sale reminders. The GIF-driven mystery builds anticipation. You have to click to find out.
what_to_steal: Use GIF animations to create mystery and urgency. Don't reveal the discount in the email. Make them click to find out. Calendar integration extends the lifecycle.

subject_line: Lemme magically transport you back to your cart. Free of charge.
title: Chubbies - Blurred Product Teaser + Thighber Monday
why_special: Blurred product images create mystery. Absurd sender name and subject line. Branded 'Thighber Monday' event. The humor IS the brand. Everything is intentionally ridiculous and it works perfectly.
what_to_steal: Blur unreleased products to build curiosity. Create your own branded shopping events. Absurdist humor can be a legitimate brand strategy.

subject_line: These colors never argue with your cabinets.
title: Clare Paint - Product IS the Design
why_special: Color swatches and painted surfaces become the visual language. The product IS the design. Subject line personifies the product ('these colors never argue'). No traditional hero imagery needed when your product is color itself.
what_to_steal: When your product is visual (paint, fabric, food), use it as the design element. Personify your product in subject lines. Skip traditional hero imagery.

title: Fenty Beauty - Minimalist Header, Maximum Impact
why_special: Minimalist header with just eyebrow copy and logo. Product photography does all the heavy lifting. Strategic white space creates a premium feel that matches the brand positioning.
what_to_steal: Strip your header to the essentials. If your product photography is strong, let it dominate. White space equals premium.

title: Patagonia - Purpose Over Product
why_special: No products. Just powerful environmental messaging and stunning landscape photography. Gold standard for mission-driven brands. Sells the cause, not the jacket. The most brand-building email in this collection.
what_to_steal: Occasionally send emails about your mission, not your products. Advocacy emails build deeper loyalty than promotional emails ever will.

title: AURA BORA - No Product, All Vibe
why_special: Omits product displays entirely. High-quality artistic images with minimal text. Humorous tone. Treats email as brand experience, not sales channel. The product is never shown and it doesn't matter.
what_to_steal: You don't always need to show the product. Build brand world through imagery and tone. Trust that brand love drives purchases.

title: Glossier - Hidden Commerce in Pure Appreciation
why_special: Heartfelt thank you email with zero visible promotional elements. But every clickable element secretly links to products. Compliments subscribers for engaging. Subtle conversion design disguised as gratitude.
what_to_steal: Appreciation emails can drive revenue without looking like they're trying. Link everything to products, but frame everything as gratitude.

subject_line: Headmaster Kel'thuzad has chosen you as his pupil, Marilia
title: Blizzard - Immersive In-Character Email
why_special: The subscriber is 'chosen' by a game character. Personalization becomes a story element. You're not reading marketing; you're receiving a message from a character. The most creative use of personalization in this collection.
what_to_steal: If your brand has characters or narrative, write emails from those characters. First-person character voice is more immersive than third-person brand voice.

title: Warby Parker - Founder Vulnerability Welcome
why_special: Shares founding struggles and vulnerabilities in the welcome sequence. Behind-the-scenes origin story. Transparency about the journey builds trust. Being real about failure is more compelling than polished success stories.
what_to_steal: Share your founding story in your welcome sequence. Include the hard parts. Vulnerability builds trust faster than perfection.

title: Dior - Haute Couture in the Inbox
why_special: Jewel tones with a single green jungle leaf to accentuate vivid shoes. Gallery-quality photography as full-width hero. The email is a digital lookbook. Every element drips sophistication. Premium brands should treat email as a luxury experience.
what_to_steal: Treat your email like a lookbook, not a flyer. Full-width editorial photography. Let the visual do the selling.

title: Resy - Spotify Wrapped for Dining
why_special: Personal dining data turned into an engaging year-in-review. Data visualization of your restaurant history. Spotify Wrapped energy but for food. Makes users feel known and valued.
what_to_steal: If you have user data, build a year-in-review. Personal data is the most engaging content you can send. Make it shareable.

title: Function of Beauty - Handwritten Apology to Your Hair
why_special: A handwritten apology letter addressed to the subscriber's hair. Breaks every email design convention. The handwritten aesthetic makes it impossible to scroll past. Addresses pain points through unique visual storytelling.
what_to_steal: Break format completely. A handwritten-style email stands out against every polished template in the inbox. Address the problem, not the product.

title: Beardbrand - Your Beard Grew 1.5 Inches
why_special: Instead of guilt-tripping lapsed users, uses a personalized fact: 'Your beard grew 1.5 inches since we last saw you.' Sent from the founder. Data-driven personalization that delights instead of nags.
what_to_steal: Re-engagement through delight, not guilt. Use time-based calculations to create surprising personalization. 'Here's what changed since you left' beats 'we miss you.'

brand: MONA (Museum of Old and New Art)
title: MONA Tasmania - Irreverent Museum Emails
why_special: Tasmania's provocative art museum brings its dark, irreverent brand voice into email. Custom Emigre fonts, moody dark palette. Refuses stuffy institutional convention with deliberately provocative language. Their tagline: 'We've got old art. New art. Wine. Restaurants. Dark corners. Nice views. Music.' One of the few cultural institutions whose emails have genuine personality.
what_to_steal: Irreverence works for institutions. Write emails like your brand actually has a personality. Dark palettes and custom typography signal confidence, not corporate.

title: Pangaia - Material Science Meets Fashion
why_special: Sustainable fashion brand that treats material science as a design element. Proprietary font family and earth-tone palette drawn from nature (mahogany, auroral olive, crocus purple). Emails weave sustainability into visual design seamlessly. 'More Pangaia, Less Impact' reflects purposeful simplicity. One of the few fashion brands where the email design itself communicates the sustainability mission through restraint.
what_to_steal: Draw your color palette from the materials you use. Earth tones and restrained design communicate sustainability more effectively than green badges and recycling icons.

title: Lucy Folk - Nomadic Luxury from Melbourne
why_special: Australian nomadic lifestyle brand that transforms creative mindset into wearable art. Montagu Slab serif typography for elegance. Warm palette of deep burgundy, turmeric, and brass metallics. Generous whitespace and carefully curated photography. Emails feel like receiving a postcard from an exotic location rather than a retail promotion. Worn by Beyonce, Solange, Zoe Kravitz.
what_to_steal: Serif fonts signal luxury in email. Warm metallics (burgundy, brass, turmeric) create richness without being loud. Treat every email as a postcard from somewhere aspirational.

title: Aesop - Minimalism as Positioning
why_special: Australian-founded skincare brand whose emails are a masterclass in restraint. Neutral palette: brown, beige, black, white. Ample breathing room. Never uses aggressive sales language. Invites customers to in-store consultations rather than pushing product. Optima font consistently. Minimalism not as a visual preference but as a positioning decision that shapes how people interpret quality.
what_to_steal: Restraint IS luxury. Never discount. Never push. Invite. A consistent neutral palette and single typeface can communicate quality more effectively than any hero banner.

subject_line: A double shot of caffeine for your booty
title: Frank Body - First-Person Brand Character Emails
why_special: Australian coffee scrub brand that built $20M on irreverent, first-person brand voice. Emails address customers as 'babe'. The brand character 'Frank' narrates all communications with cheeky humor. Consistently playful tone creates urgency without being pushy. 2.2M units sold to 149 countries, largely through word-of-mouth amplified by this distinctive email personality.
what_to_steal: Create a brand character and write every email from their perspective. First-person voice ('I' not 'we') feels more personal. Cheeky beats corporate every time.
conversion_data: $20M business built largely on email-driven word-of-mouth. 2.2M units, 149 countries.

title: Liquid Death - Punk Rock Water Newsletter
why_special: Canned water brand with heavy metal and punk aesthetics. Skull imagery, dark colors, edgy typography. Signup promises subscribers will be 'brainwashed by Liquid Death marketing through rare (but hilarious) emails.' Intentionally anti-corporate in the most sterile product category. Darkly humorous content. Proves even the most boring product can have the most distinctive email identity.
what_to_steal: The more boring your product category, the more room you have to be wild with brand voice. Promise subscribers something unexpected at signup. Under-send and over-deliver.

title: Fly By Jing - Emails That Taste Like Chili Oil
why_special: Chinese chili sauce brand with emails that mirror product intensity. Bright red CTAs, product tags, 'low stock' notifications matching the fiery aesthetic. Visual language borrows from Chinese design traditions while feeling entirely modern. A food brand where the email design literally evokes the taste of the product.
what_to_steal: Your email should feel like your product tastes, smells, or feels. Match the intensity of your brand to the intensity of your design. Cultural design traditions are an untapped differentiator.

title: Omsom - Every Transactional Email Is Different
why_special: Asian-founded DTC pantry brand where every transactional email has a different look and feel rather than standard templates. Order confirmation through delivery notification each tells a different story. Welcome email features a personal founder letter. Turns the boring post-purchase flow into a brand discovery journey. Proves operational emails are an underused canvas.
what_to_steal: Don't template your transactional emails. Make each one (confirmation, shipping, delivery) a different brand moment. The post-purchase flow is your most-opened email sequence. Treat it that way.
